coredata.dc:title: Cellulose nanofiber-based multifunctional composite films integrated with zinc doped-grapefruit peel-based carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Su Jung
Ha, Seong Yong
Shin, Gye Hwa
Kim, Jun Tae
coredata.dc:description: This study aimed to develop a multifunctional active composite film to extend the shelf life of minced pork. The composite film was prepared by incorporating zinc-doped grapefruit peel-derived carbon quantum dots (Zn-GFP-CD) into a cellulose nanofiber (CNF) matrix. The resulting film significantly improved UV-blocking properties from 39.0 % to 85.7 % while maintaining the film transparency. Additionally, the CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% composite film exhibits strong antioxidant activity with ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activities of 99.8 % and 77.4 %, respectively. The composite film also showed excellent antibacterial activity against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. When used in minced pork packaging, the composite films effectively inhibit bacterial growth, maintaining bacterial levels below 7 Log CFU/g after 15 days and sustaining a red color over a 21-day storage period. Additionally, a significant reduction in the lipid oxidation of the minced pork was observed. These CNF/Zn-GFP-CD composite films have a great potential for active food packaging applications to extend shelf life and maintain the visual quality of packaged meat.

head.ce:title: Cellulose nanofiber-based multifunctional composite films integrated with zinc doped-grapefruit peel-based carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aimed to develop a multifunctional active composite film to extend the shelf life of minced pork. The composite film was prepared by incorporating zinc-doped grapefruit peel-derived carbon quantum dots (Zn-GFP-CD) into a cellulose nanofiber (CNF) matrix. The resulting film significantly improved UV-blocking properties from 39.0 % to 85.7 % while maintaining the film transparency. Additionally, the CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% composite film exhibits strong antioxidant activity with ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activities of 99.8 % and 77.4 %, respectively. The composite film also showed excellent antibacterial activity against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. When used in minced pork packaging, the composite films effectively inhibit bacterial growth, maintaining bacterial levels below 7 Log CFU/g after 15 days and sustaining a red color over a 21-day storage period. Additionally, a significant reduction in the lipid oxidation of the minced pork was observed. These CNF/Zn-GFP-CD composite films have a great potential for active food packaging applications to extend shelf life and maintain the visual quality of packaged meat.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the term “white pollution”, which refers to the growing environmental problems caused by the spread of non-biodegradable petroleum-based plastic packaging materials, has received a lot of attention [1]. Environmental deterioration due to “white pollution” can further deepen ecological destruction and pose a potential threat to human health [2]. As a result, there is a growing trend towards developing and utilizing environmentally friendly packaging materials based on biopolymers from petroleum-based plastics [3].

Among biopolymers, cellulose has emerged as an attractive environmentally friendly alternative to petroleum-based plastics, with global production exceeding 1 trillion kilograms [4,5]. In particular, cellulose nanofiber (CNF) is attracting great attention in various application fields such as eco-friendly food packaging for fruits and vegetables due to its biodegradability, good mechanical strength, and excellent gas barrier property [6]. CNF also offers the unique advantage of being seamlessly integrated with various functional substances that exhibit antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV protection properties [7,8]. These sustainability and performance advantages make CNF a promising candidate for eco-friendly materials in the packaging industry. However, the ongoing task of these efforts is to provide functionality such as antioxidant and antimicrobial properties to biopolymer-based composite films. This is important to ensure food quality and extend shelf life by mitigating spoilage and product deterioration caused by microorganisms [9].

In response to these challenges, active packaging containing antibacterial and antioxidant agents has emerged as a promising solution to protect foods from microbial contamination and lipid oxidation [10–12]. These systems are designed to respond to the challenges faced while transporting perishable foods by extending shelf life, preventing spoilage, and maintaining product freshness [13]. Various studies have been conducted on the development of functional nanomaterials for application to active packaging films [14,15].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or carbon dots (CDs) have recently gained prominence as potential fillers in food packaging due to their environmental sustainability, chemical stability, antioxidant and antibacterial activities, ease of synthesis and surface functionalization, compatibility, and hydrophilicity [16,17]. CDs can be synthesized using various food processing by-products and natural resources from agriculture [18]. Grapefruit peel (GFP) is one such valuable natural resource. GFP is rich in polyphenolic compounds such as naringin and limonin, which are known for their antibacterial and antioxidant properties [19]. In contrast, by-products such as potato peels, banana peels, and coffee grounds lack their inherent antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. Taking advantage of the high antioxidant and antibacterial potential of GFP, GFP-derived CDs (GFP-CDs) can serve as multifunctional nanofillers for active composite films [20]. These properties make GFP-CDs a promising candidate for applications requiring antimicrobial and antioxidant capabilities, providing a sustainable and effective alternative to a variety of industries, including active packaging and biomedical applications. Additionally, the derivation of CDs from agricultural by-products such as GFP emphasizes the utilization of renewable resources and waste reduction. This environmentally conscious technology serves the important goal of strengthening efforts to address “while pollution” by ameliorating the environmental degradation associated with traditional petroleum-based plastics.

Zinc is recognized for its cost-effectiveness and granted Generally Recognized as Safe (GRAS) status by the United States Food and Drug Administration (US FDA) [21]. It is widely used in film forming systems to create active food packaging due to its various advantages such as broad antimicrobial properties, economic viability, non-toxic properties, and favorable photocatalytic properties [22,23]. Additionally, adding zinc to CDs serves to increase the stability of the final structure by fixing the surface functional groups of the CDs through interaction with metal ions [24]. Therefore, the GFP-CDs was doped with zinc (Zn-GFP-CDs) to improve its functional properties and synthesized via a hydrothermal method.

The primary aim of this investigation was to create a multifunctional active composite film by incorporating Zn-GFP-CDs into a CNF matrix. Firstly, a comprehensive evaluation of the impact of Zn-GFP-CDs on both physicochemical and functional properties of CNF-based composite films was performed. Additionally, the potential for extending the shelf life was assessed by applying the developed CNF film to the packaging of minced pork.

Fresh grapefruit and minced pork were acquired from a local market in Seoul, Korea. CNF with a width of 10–20 nm, length of 3–350 μm, and viscosity of 2000 cPs at 0.75 wt% was provided from Moorim Co. Ltd. (Ulsan, Korea). ρ-Anisidine and 2,2′-azino-bis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), sodium thiosulfate, trichloroacetic acid, 1,1,3,3-tetramethoxypropane, and thiobarbituric acid were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The other reagents, including zinc nitrate hexahydrate, isooctane, potassium iodide, acetic acid, and n-hexane, were obtained from Daejung Chemicals & Materials Cooperation (Siheung, Korea). Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 13565), Salmonella enterica (ATCC 14028), Escherichia coli O157: H7 (ATCC 43895), and Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 15313) were sourced from the Korean Collection for Type Culture (Jeongeup, Korea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of zinc doped-grapefruit peel-based carbon dots (Zn-GFP-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Zn-GFP-CDs were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal method using GFP with zinc nitrate hexahydrate as the zinc source. Briefly, GFP was dried at 50 °C for 24 h, ground, and 1 g GFP powder was dispersed in 50 mL distilled water. Then, 0.1 g of zinc nitrate hexahydrate was added to this solution. This solution was added to a stainless steel reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) and reacted in a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the supernatant was filtered using a membrane filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm to remove impurities. For comparison, GFP-CDs were also synthesized using the same procedure, although no Zn source was added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cellulose nanofiber/Zn-GFP-CDs (CNF/Zn-GFP-CDs) composite films
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare CNF-based composite films (CNF-based films), 2 g of CNF and 1.2 g of glycerol were added to 200 mL of distilled water and dissolved using a magnetic stirrer at 400 rpm for 2 h. Then, 0, 0.02, 0.06, and 0.10 g of Zn-GFP-CDs (corresponding to 0, 1, 3, and 5 wt% relative to the CNF) was added to the solution while stirring was continued. Finally, the solution was poured onto a Teflon-coated glass plate and dried at room temperature for 72 h. After drying, the films were conditioned at 25 °C and 50 % relative humidity before testing their mechanical and physical properties. The prepared films were designated as follows: neat CNF (0 wt% Zn-GFP-CDs), CNF/Zn-GFP-CD1% (1 wt% Zn-GFP-CDs), CNF/Zn-GFP-CD3% (3 wt% Zn-GFP-CDs), and CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% (5 wt% Zn-GFP-CDs).

The antioxidant activities of GFP-CDs, Zn-GFP-CDs, and CNF-based composite films were performed through ABTS and DPPH assays. The formation of ABTS radicals occurred through a chemical oxidation process using potassium persulfate. ABTS was dissolved in a 2.45 mM potassium persulfate solution to obtain a concentration of 7 mM. The mixture was then incubated overnight in the dark to produce blue-green ABTS radicals. Afterwards, it was diluted with distilled water to obtain an absorbance of 0.70 ± 0.02 at 734 nm to prepare an ABTS radical solution. Then, 20 μL of sample prepared at a concentration range of 10–100 μg/mL was mixed with 180 μL of this ABTS radical solution in a 96-microwell plate. After incubation in the dark for 2 min, absorbance was measured at 734 nm using a multi-detection microplate reader. The ABTS radical scavenging rate was calculated using the following Eq. (1):
(1)
Radical scavenging rate
%

A c

A s

× 100
where Ac and As were the absorbances of the control and test sample, respectively.

Additionally, DPPH radical solution was prepared by dissolving DPPH in methanol at a concentration of 0.2 mM. Afterwards, 160 μL of sample prepared in the concentration range of 10–100 μg/mL was mixed with 40 μL of this DPPH radical solution in a 96-microwell plate. After incubation in the dark for 2 h, absorbance was measured at 517 nm using a multi-detection microplate reader. The DPPH radical scavenging rate was also calculated using the above Eq. (1)

The antimicrobial activities of GFP-CDs, Zn-GFP-CDs, and CNF-based films were performed against E. coli, S. enterica, L. monocytogenes, and S. aureus. The antibacterial activity of GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs was evaluated using agar well diffusion assay. In this assay, the surface of agar plate was initially inoculated with bacteria at a concentration of 106 CFU/mL. A sterile punch was then used to create an 8 mm diameter well on the agar surface. Afterwards, 200 μL of CDs samples was introduced into each well. The plates were then incubated at 37 °C for 12 h and the formation of inhibition zones was observed.

Furthermore, Minimal Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) and Minimal Bactericidal Concentration (MBC) values for both GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs were determined using the Resazurin-based 96-well plate microdilution method. Briefly, 200 μL of CDs samples ranging in concentration from 10 to 1000 μg/mL were injected into each well and added to 50 μL of bacterial broth containing approximately 105 CFU/mL. After incubation at 37 °C for 12 h, 30 μL of resazurin solution (0.015 %) was added to each well and incubated for 30 min. The initial blue color of the resazurin solution changes to pink color when it interacts with live bacterial cells in the broth, serving as a visual indicator of bacterial growth. Wells with no color change (blue resazurin color unchanged) were scored as MIC value.

The antibacterial activity of CNF-based composite films was evaluated using Time-Kill assay. Tested bacteria were incubated at 37 °C for 16 h and diluted to 105–106 CFU/mL with broth. The CNF-based composite films (200 mg) was added to 20 mL of the broth solution in a 100 mL sterilized conical flask and incubated at 37 °C with moderate shaking. Every 3 h interval, 100 μL of culture from each flask was spread onto agar plates. Total viable colonies for each bacterial strain were calculated as Log CFU/mL.

The color parameters (L, a, and b values) of the CNF-based composite films and neat CNF film were measured using a Chroma meter (Konica Minolta, CR-400, Tokyo, Japan) with a standard white plate (L = 97.75, a = −0.49, and b = 1.96) as a background. The total color difference (ΔE) was calculated using the following Eq. (2):
(2)
∆ E =
ΔL
2
+
Δa
2
+
Δb
2
where ΔL, Δa, and Δb are the differences of L-value, a-value, and b-value between the CNF/Zn-GFP-CDs and the neat CNF film, respectively.

The light transmittance of the CNF-based composite films was determined at 280 nm (T280) for UV-light barrier and 660 nm (T660) for visible light (transparency) using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP series UV/Vis, Seoul, Korea).

The WVP of CNF-based composite films was determined using a gravimetric method (ASTM E96-95). First, a WVP cup with an inner diameter of 6.8 cm and a height of 2.5 cm was filled with 18 mL of distilled water. Film samples (7.5 cm × 7.5 cm) was then attached and tightly sealed to ensure no leaks. The assembled WVP cup was then placed in a temperature and humidity chamber maintained at 25 °C and 50 % RH. The weight of the cup was measured every hour for total 8 h. Water vapor transmission rate (WVTR, g/m2·s) was determined by analyzing the slope of the weight loss curve observed in the WVP cup over a specified time period. The WVP of the film was calculated using the following Eq. (3):
(3)
WVP =
WVTR × L
/ Δp
where L was the thickness of the CNF-based composite film (m), and Δp was the partial vapor pressure difference (Pa) across the CNF-based composite film.

The mechanical properties of CNF-based composite films were evaluated utilizing a Universal Testing Machine (AG-10kNX Plus, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) according to ASTM D882–88. The rectangular strips of film samples were cut into 10 mm × 150 mm using a precision cutting machine (QM 130N, QMESYS, Uiwangsi, Korea), and the thickness was measured at five random plance of the specimen using a micrometer caliper (model MDC-25 MJ, Mitutoyo Co., Kanagawa, Japan). Tests were performed with an initial grip distance of 50 mm and a cross-head speed of 10 mm/min and evaluated tensile strength (TS), Young's modulus (YM), and elongation at break (EB).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Packaging application of CNF/Zn-GFP-CDs composite films for pork preservation
ce:section.ce:para: Minced pork was used to verify the efficacy of CNF-based composite films for active packaging application. First, neat CNF film and CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film were cut into 7 cm × 8 cm, and 20 g of minced pork was treated with UV rays on both sides of the films for 15 min and then packaged. The packaged minced pork was placed in a low-density polyethylene (LDPE) bag, vacuum-sealed, and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Color change, bacterial growth, and lipid oxidation of minced pork were evaluated at 3-day intervals during 21-day storage.

TVC count method was used to measure the bacterial growth of the packed minced pork. For this purpose, 5 g of minced pork was homogenized in 45 mL of 0.1 % peptone water for 3 min using a stomacher (BagMixer 400, Interscience, France). After serial dilution, homogeneous aliquots (100 μL) were spread on nutrient agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 48 h. Results were expressed as Log CFU/g based on colony counts.

Briefly, minced pork (5 g) was extracted by vortexing for 2 min in 20 mL of a 10 % (w/v) trichloroacetic acid solution. After centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min, the supernatant was filtrated using Whatman No. 1 filter paper. Filtered solution (5 mL) was mixed with 5 mL of a 20 mM thiobarbituric acid aqueous solution, and the mixture was reacted at 95 °C for 40 min. Afterwards, the samples were cooled using ice for 15 min and the absorbance was recorded at 532 nm. Results are expressed as mg Malonaldehyde (MDA)/kg derived from a standard curve covering the 1,1,3,3-tetramethoxypropane concentration range from 0 to 10 mM.

Lipids were extracted from minced pork and peroxide value (PV) and ρ-anisidine value (ρ-AV) were determined. n-Hexane (100 mL) was added to 20 g of minced port and ground for 1 min. Afterwards, the mixture was filtered using a Whatman No. 1 filter paper and evaporated at 70 °C dry oven. PV and ρ-AV were measured according to the AOCS official method (CD 8b-90) and AOCS official method (CD 18–90), respectively.

GFP-CDs, Zn-GFP-CDs, and CNF-based composite films were characterized at least three times, and the data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis of experimental data was performed using SPSS version 26.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was applied and the significance of each mean value was determined using Duncan's multiple range tests at the significance level set at p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis properties, FT-IR spectra, and photoluminescence (PL) spectra
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the photographs of GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs under visible, UV light, UV/visible light absorption spectra, and FT-IR spectra. As a result of the quantum confinement effect, the original brown color of the GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs solutions was converted to blue light when exposed to UV excitation at 365 nm (Fig. 1a,b). GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs show a distinct phenomenon of rapid color transformation upon UV irradiation [16]. UV light energizes the CD structure by promoting electrons to a higher energy state and then back to a lower energy state, releasing energy in the process. The released energy appears as fluorescence, which represents the fluorescence phenomenon of CDs under UV light [25]. Additionally, the GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs solutions exhibited an absorption peak at 290 nm due to the n–π⁎ transition of the CO or C–OH bond within the CDs (Fig. 1c).

As shown in Fig. 1d, the absorption peak observed at approximately 3250 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of CDs is attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds. Additionally, the absorption band around 2920 cm−1 represents stretching vibrations associated with CH bonds within the structure of CDs [26]. The absorption band around 1700 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the CO bond [27]. Moreover, combined vibrations associated with O–C–H and C–O–H groups are evident in the absorption band observed in the region of 1350 to 1580 cm−1 due to the surface functional groups of CDs [28]. The absorption peak observed at 1045 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC bonds and bending vibrations of aromatic CH bonds [29]. The absorption peak around 1190 cm−1 indicates the deformation of CH and OH bonds, due to surface functional groups of CDs structure [27]. After Zn-doping, a pronounced stretching vibration of Zn–OH bond appeared at approximately 1000 cm−1 [30]. Additionally, discernible bands appeared at 831 cm−1 and 1312 cm−1, which were identified as characteristic vibrational modes of nitrate ions [31]. The presence of these new peaks shows that the GFP-CDs structure was successfully doped with zinc.

Fig. 2 shows the PL spectra of the GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs at the excitation wavelengths. Both samples exhibited strong and broad emission peaks within the blue spectrum. Moreover, the emission characteristics showed an excitation wavelength-dependent profile with a progressive redshift from 450 nm to 500 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 450 nm. For GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs, the maximum PL emission intensity was recorded at approximately 450 nm when excited at wavelengths of 350 nm and 340 nm, respectively. GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs exhibit unique PL properties due to their specific structural characteristics. These CDs mainly consist of a carbon-rich core decorated with abundant surface functional groups as a result of the carbonization process of GFP precursors [32]. The PL of CDs is generally attributed to surface-related electronic states, molecular states, surface functional groups, and surface energy traps, as well as radiative recombination of excitons in the core (carbon-core state) [33]. Previous studies have shown that it is commonly observed that CDs exhibit unique changes in their emission spectra as the excitation wavelength is varied. In particular, longer excitation wavelengths tend to shift the PL spectrum to the red [34]. The tunable fluorescence emission of CDs results from several fluorescence parameters, including carbon source, synthesis method, different sizes and shapes, as well as the presence of different emission sites and functional groups on the surface [18].

The particle sizes of GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs were recorded by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the results are shown in Fig. S1 (Supplement). Both GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs were monodisperse with spherical shapes <15 nm in diameter (in the quantum range). Das et al. [35] reported that the size of carbon dot particles ranges from 1 to 20 nm depending on the synthesis method and material.

The particle sizes of GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs were recorded by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the results are shown in Fig. S1 (Supplement). Both GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs were monodisperse with spherical shapes <15 nm in diameter (in the quantum range). Das et al. [35] reported that the size of carbon dot particles ranges from 1 to 20 nm depending on the synthesis method and material.

The XPS analysis results for GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs are shown in Figs. 3 and 4
, respectively. The survey spectrum of GFP-CDs (Fig. 3a) showed peaks of C ls (72.08 %), N ls (1.68 %), and O ls (26.14 %) at 284, 399, and 531 eV, respectively. In the case of Zn-GFP-CDs (Fig. 4a), the peaks of C ls (68.43 %), N ls (1.52 %), O ls (28.83 %), and Zn 2p (1.23 %) were recorded at 284, 399, 531, and 1021 eV, respectively. Figs. 3b–d and 4b–e show the high-resolution XPS spectra for each individual element. In the C 1 s spectrum (Figs. 3b and 4b), three distinct peaks are identified, located at 284.7 eV, 285.9 eV, and 287.5 eV. These can be attributed to C=C–C, CO, and CO species [36,37]. High-resolution spectra corresponding to the N 1 s spectra of GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs confirm the presence of NH2 (399.0 eV), pyridine-N (400.2 eV), and quaternary-N (401.3 eV) bonds (Figs. 3c and 4c) [38]. The spectra associated with O 1 s in Figs. 3d and 4d can be resolved into three peaks at 530.7, 532.1, and 534.1 eV. These peaks correspond to group bonds, CO, CO, and C–OOH [39]. Finally, in Fig. 4e, three deconvoluted peaks of Zn 2p are exhibited at core levels of 1020.2, 1021.8, and 1044.1 eV. These peaks correspond to Zn 2p(3/2)s, Zn 2p3/2, and Zn 2p1/2, respectively [40]. In addition to the FT-IR results, the XPS analysis results further confirmed that the desired heteroatoms were incorporated into the GFP-CDs, confirming the successful doping of zinc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and antioxidant activities of GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The results of the agar well diffusion assay and resazurin-based 96-well microdilution method for GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs for S. aureus, S. enterica, E. coli, and L. monocytogenes are presented in Table 1 . GFP-CDs showed inhibition zones of 23.14 ± 0.49 mm, 22.06 ± 0.39 mm, 17.24 ± 0.60 mm, and 16.44 ± 0.46 mm for S. aureus, S. enterica, E. coli, and L. monocytogenes, respectively. After zinc doping, a significant improvement in the inhibition zones was observed for all tested bacteria. Specifically, the inhibition zones for S. aureus, S. enterica, E. coli, and L. monocytogenes showed values of 24.30 ± 0.56 mm, 23.48 ± 0.82 mm, 17.65 ± 0.23 mm, and 17.59 ± 0.24 mm, respectively.

Additionally, the MIC/MBC values of GFP-CDs are 100/200 μg/mL for S. aureus, 200/500 μg/mL for S. enterica, 350/600 μg/mL for E. coli, and 300/600 μg/mL for L. monocytogenes. Consistent with the agar well diffusion method results, zinc doping resulted in a noticeable decrease in MIC and MBC values for all tested bacteria. The MIC/MBC values of Zn-GFP-CDs for S. aureus were lowered to 25/25 μg/mL, while those for S. enterica were adjusted to 100/150 μg/mL. Similarly, the MIC/MBC values for E. coli and L. monocytogenes decreased to 200/400 μg/mL and 200/450 μg/mL, respectively.

Antioxidant properties of GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs by ABTS and DPPH assays are summarized in Table 2 . ABTS radical scavenging rate of GFP-CDs increased from 10.33 ± 3.14 % to 91.58 ± 0.55 % as the concentration increased from 10 to 100 μg/mL. After zinc doping, the ABTS radical scavenging rate was significantly (p < 0.05) improved, increasing from 26.54 ± 3.21 % to 93.99 ± 0.24 % as the concentration increased from 10 to 100 μg/mL. ABTS radical scavenging rate is quantified as RC50, which represents the quantity necessary to reduce ABTS radical presence by 50 %. Smaller RC50 values indicate higher ABTS radical scavenging ability, which reflects higher activity in neutralizing these radicals [41]. The RC50 values of GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs for ABTS radicals scavenging were 47.23 ± 1.16 μg/mL and 21.58 ± 0.40 μg/mL, respectively. Similarly, the radical scavenging rate of GFP-CDs by the DPPH assay significantly (p < 0.05) increased from 39.41 ± 0.39 % to 50.57 ± 0.78 % at the concentrations from 10 to 100 μg/mL. After zinc doping, the DPPH radical scavenging rate increased from 46.24 ± 0.39 % to 56.95 ± 1.18 % within the same concentration range, showing significant (p < 0.05) improvement. Quantification of the DPPH radical scavenging rate by RC50 measured values of 84.22 ± 2.86 μg/mL for GFP-CDs and 30.83 ± 1.32 μg/mL for Zn-GFP-CDs. Antioxidant and antibacterial effects of GFP-CDs and Zn-GFP-CDs appear to be due to various phenolic compounds in grapefruit, including naringin, naringenin, and limonin [19]. It has been also well known that CDs have significant antioxidant and antibacterial activities [24]. Additionally, the presence of Zn ions induces stress, resulting in deformation of the bacterial cytoplasmic membrane and subsequent changes in cell morphology. Penetration of Zn ions into the cell wall causes cytoplasmic leakage, ultimately leading to cell death [42]. The stronger antibacterial properties of Zn-GFP-CDs indicate their potential for future applications in food preservation.

SEM images (Fig. 5a, b) allow to visually evaluate the dispersion of Zn-GFP-CDs particles within the surface and cross-section of CNF-based composite films. Neat CNF film exhibited a smooth, intact surface with characteristic curved and wavy clusters. The addition of Zn-GFP-CDs resulted in a dense, compact, uniform, and smooth surface without voids or cracks, indicating strong compatibility between Zn-GFP-CDs and the CNF matrix. Additionally, cross-sectional images of the composite film show a consistent internal structure without any signs of ruptures, pores, or cavities. The study results suggest that CDs are evenly distributed within the CNF matrix and that the film components exhibit good distribution and compatibility.

The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 5c show the chemical interactions and changes observed in the CNF-based composite films upon the addition of Zn-GFP-CDs. Within neat CNF film, O − H stretching is identified as a distinctive band at 3365 cm−1, while CH stretching is represented by a small hump at 2903 cm−1 [43]. Additionally, a distinct peak at 1615 cm−1 and accompanying shoulder bands in the range of 1307–1393 cm−1 were detected, indicating OH stretch vibrations [8] and CO stretch due to the presence of absorbed water molecules and hydroxymethyl group (CH2OH), respectively [44]. The band around 1153 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the CC ring, while the band at 1089 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of the C–O–C pyranose ring of cellulose [45]. Moreover, the peak observed at 1010 cm−1 can be attributed to the asymmetric stretching of CO bonds in CNF [7]. The spectra of CNF/Zn-GFP-CD1%, CNF/Zn-GFP-CD3%, and CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% composite films showed very similar peak patterns to neat CNF film without any noticeable wavelength changes. The similarity of the spectra can be attributed to the relatively low content of loaded Zn-GFP-CDs and the result of overlap of CH, OH, CC, and CO stretching vibrations within the same IR spectra. This is consistent with other related studies [8,16,46]. Therefore, it can be inferred that the peak intensity of the CNF/Zn-GFP-CDs composite films spectra did not change significantly except for a small change in the frequency region [1]. This observation supports the notion of uniform integration and satisfactory compatibility of Zn-GFP-CDs within the CNF matrix.

The optical properties of food packaging films play an important role in shaping the visual appeal of packaged foods and have a direct impact on consumer acceptance [47]. Table 3 provides the visual appearance, color parameter, and light transmittance of the CNF-based composite films. Neat CNF film showed a colorless and transparent appearance, while CNF/Zn-GFP-CD composite films showed a progressively deeper yellowish color as the concentration of Zn-GFP-CDs increased. The addition of Zn-GFP-CDs significantly (p < 0.05) reduced the brightness (L-value) from 91.69 ± 0.31 to 86.93 ± 0.33 compared to neat CNF film. In comparison, the yellowness (b-value) of the composite films was increased from −0.15 ± 0.05 to 9.99 ± 0.23 due to the brownish-yellow color of Zn-GFP-CDs. As a result, the overall color difference (ΔE) increased significantly (p < 0.05) due to changes in L and b-values.

Exposure to light can cause nutrient degradation, oxidation, and formation of harmful compounds in foods [48]. Therefore, transparency and UV protection are important properties of food packaging films to protect food quality. The transparency and UV-barrier properties of the CNF-based composite films were evaluated by measuring light transmittance at 660 nm (T660) and 280 nm (T280). Neat CNF film showed notable levels of transmittance with values of 61.00 ± 1.31 % in the UV region at 280 nm and 84.95 ± 0.84 % in the visible region at 660 nm, respectively. As the Zn-GFP-CDs concentration increased from 1 % to 5 %, the UV transmittance decreased significantly (p < 0.05). Specifically, the UV transmittance of CNF-based films significantly (p < 0.05) decreased from 43.39 ± 1.95 % to 14.29 ± 0.43 % with increasing Zn-GFP-CDs concentrations. However, it had a minimal impact on transparency of the composite films with T660 values ranged from 82.13 ± 0.02 % to 84.54 ± 0.13 %. The increase in UV barrier properties by incorporating Zn-GFP-CDs can be explained by the SEM results shown in Fig. 5a & b. Due to the aggregation of Zn-GFP-CDs, it became clear that the interstitial spaces within the CNF matrix were filled with small particles [1]. This structural change significantly affected the films, reducing its UV light transmittance. Another possible explanation for this phenomenon could be the light scattering and light absorption properties of CDs [18]. Additionally, the high content of polyphenolic compounds from GFP, which is known to be rich in aromatic groups, contribute to the UV/Visible light absorption properties of the composite films [24]. A similar UV-blocking effect was also observed in CNF films containing glucose-derived CDs [8]. Distinguished by excellent UV-blocking properties, processed CNF-based films can proficiently protect packaged foods from photo-oxidation and photodegradation reactions triggered by UV radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties and water vaper permeability
ce:section.ce:para: Packaging films must have strong mechanical properties to ensure safe storage, transportation, and handling of food and to prevent breakage or leakage. The thickness, mechanical properties, and WVP of CNF-based films are shown in Table 4 . The thickness of CNF/Zn-GFP-CD1% film is 28.05 ± 2.02 μm and not significantly (p > 0.05) different from that of neat CNF film (28.43 ± 1.94 μm). By adding a high concentration of Zn-GFP-CDs, the thickness was significantly (p < 0.05) increased by including solid-dense components [49]. Specifically, the thickness value increased to 30.45 ± 1.63 μm in CNF/Zn-GFP-CD3% film and 32.48 ± 1.37 μm in CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film.

Tensile strength (TS), Young's modulus (YM), and elongation at break (EB) were used as important parameters to evaluate the mechanical properties of CNF-based films. Neat CNF film exhibited TS of 70.00 ± 5.85 MPa, YM of 4.51 ± 0.47 GPa, and EB of 10.50 ± 1.21 %, exhibiting properties of strong (high TS), stiff (high YM), and adequate flexibility (adequate EB) property. The inclusion of Zn-GFP-CDs at concentrations up to 3 wt% did not significantly (p > 0.05) affect the TS of the CNF-based film, which remained within the range of 70.00 ± 5.85 MPa to 71.45 ± 5.02 MPa. However, when 5 wt% Zn-GFP-CDs was added, TS slightly decreased to a value of 59.68 ± 3.78 MPa. The YM of neat CNF film was 4.51 ± 0.47 GPa and slightly increased to 4.85 ± 0.31 GPa and 4.84 ± 0.34 GPa by adding 1 wt% Zn-GFP-CDs and 3 wt% Zn-GFP-CDs, respectively. However, the YM of CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film significantly (p < 0.05) decreased to 4.11 ± 0.24 GPa. The addition of the fillers affected the TS and YM of the composite films. This effect may occur because intermolecular interactions within the CNF matrix are potentially disrupted, making the structure less organized and weaker when the polymer chains are disrupted [1]. However, the incorporation of Zn-GFP-CDs did not significantly (p > 0.05) change the EB of the CNF-based films, ranging from 9.57 ± 0.97 % to 10.50 ± 1.21 %. It is noteworthy that the strength and stiffness of the CNF-based films decreased with addition of high concentration of Zn-GFP-Cds, but remained relatively high compared to other single biopolymer-based films, such as agar-based film [50], carrageenan-based film [51], and chitosan-based film [1]. These findings are consistent with the results reported by Konwar et al. [52], where the decrease of TS in chitosan-based films upon addition of CDs was shown to be due to excessive loading or aggregation of CDs at higher concentrations. Additionally, Das et al. [53] also reported that the incorporation of CDs into carrageenan-based films reduced TS.

The WVP of composite films varies markedly depending on the nature of the filler and its interaction with the polymer matrix [54]. The passage of water vapor through the film is controlled by a series of complex physical mechanisms. This involves adsorption of water vapor on the film surface, diffusion of water molecules, and evaporation of water on the other side of the film [16]. The WVP of CNF-based films was found to significantly (p < 0.05) increase from 5.45 ± 0.31 × 10−10 g m/m2·Pa·s to 7.95 ± 0.24 × 10−10 g m/m2·Pa·s with increasing Zn-GFP-CDs concentration. The increase in WVP can be attributed to the establishment of complex pathways that promote water vapor diffusion [55]. These pathways are formed bu. incorporating hydrophilic Zn-GFP-CDs within the CNF matrix.

Food oxidation is a series of reaction that initiate the production of free radicals that ultimately lead to undesirable outcomes such as rancidity, nutrient degradation, discoloration, and off-flavors. These factors generally reduce the overall quality and shelf life of foods [42]. The results of ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activities through direct contact with CNF-based films are shown in Fig. 6 . Neat CNF film showed negligible antioxidant activity of 1.47 ± 0.83 % for ABTS scavenging rate and 1.88 ± 0.26 % for DPPH scavenging rate, which is consistent with the results reported by Roy & Rhim [56]. CNF/Zn-GFP-CDs films showed increased free radical scavenging rate for ABTS and DPPH with increasing concentration of Zn-GFP-CDs. Specifically, the ABTS radical scavenging rate values increased to 58.75 ± 4.20 % in CNF/Zn-GFP-CD1% film and 99.81 ± 0.22 % in CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film, while the DPPH radical scavenging rate values increased to 17.46 ± 0.65 % in CNF/Zn-GFP-CD1% film and 77.44 ± 1.99 % in CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film. The scavenging activity against ABTS•+ and DPPH• is achieved through proton transfer from the -OH and amide groups of CDs to DPPH-H and ABTS-H [57]. Additionally, the electron donating properties exhibited by oxygen atoms of zinc doping can be attributed to the antioxidant properties of the fabricated composite films [58]. The observed higher ABTS radical scavenging rate compared to the DPPH radical scavenging rate can be attributed to the hydrophilic nature of the CNF film, the release of Zn-GFP-CDs into the ABTS aqueous solution, and the enhanced interaction with ABTS radicals and augmenting their scavenging activity [13].

Antibacterial packaging films play a vital role as a protective barrier, significantly reducing the risk of bacterial contamination and maintaining the overall quality of foods. The antibacterial activity of CNF-based films is shown in Fig. 7 . As expected, neat CNF film showed no antibacterial activity. On the other hand, CNF/Zn-GFP-CD films showed significant antibacterial activity against S. aureus, S. enterica, E. coli, and L. monocytogenes. CNF/Zn-GFP-CD3% and CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% films completely inhibited the growth of all bacteria tested within 9 h and 12 h after exposure, respectively. CNF/Zn-GFP-CD1% film reduced the colony count of S. aureus and L. monocytogenes by approximately 7 Log CFU/mL and for S. enterica and E. coli by approximately 6 Log CFU/mL and 6.5 Log CFU/mL after 12 h of exposure, respectively. Studies have shown that Zn-GFP-CDs exhibit higher efficacy in inhibiting the growth of Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus and L. monocytogenes) compared to Gram-negative bacteria (S. enterica and E. coli) due to structural differences. Grapefruit consists of numerous polyphenolic compounds, such as naringin and limonin, which are known for their antimicrobial properties [19]. Its mode of antimicrobial action involves disruption of cell membranes and potentially binds to cellular proteins, impairing their function [59,60]. The most widely accepted hypothesis for the antibacterial action of CDs is the generation of ROS, which generates singlet oxygen, superoxide anions, and hydroxyl free radicals [54]. Additionally, nano-sized CDs can penetrate the cell wall of bacteria and disrupt cell functions, metabolic processes, protein degradation, and DNA replication, disrupting cellular machinery and ultimately causing cell lysis [16]. The fabricated CNF-based films with strong antibacterial properties can effectively prevent microbial contamination, potentially extending the shelf life of food.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CNF/Zn-GFP-CDs for pork preservation
ce:section.ce:para: Minced pork was chosen to compare the effectiveness of neat CNF film and CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film for active packaging application. Fig. 8a shows changes in the visual appearance of wrapped pork after 21 days of storage. Minced pork wrapped with CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film showed a noticeabe improvement in red color compared to that wrapped with neat CNF film. Additionally, the color change of the minced meat during storage was shown in Fig. 8b. The a-value of meat is used as a convenient metric for monitoring changes in color quality of red meat during storage. Consistent with the visual appearance results, the a-value of minced pork wrapped with CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film was significantly (p < 0.05) higher than that of meat wrapped with neat CNF film. Studies have shown that the UV blocking and antioxidant effects of CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film alleviate discoloration caused by pigment (myoglobin) oxidation in meat [61].

The antibacterial effectiveness of CNF-based films are shown in Fig. 8c. The TVC is a widely used method to quantify microbial contamination levels [46]. The International Commission on Microbiological Specifications for Foods (ICMSF) recommends that meat with a TVC value exceeding 7 log CFU/mL be considered spoiled [62]. The initial TVC of minced pork was approximately 3.96 Log CFU/g, but increased rapidly to approximately 10.20 Log CFU/g and 9.13 Log CFU/g after 21 days in minced pork wrapped with neat CNF film and CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film, respectively. However, minced pork wrapped with CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film showed significantly (p < 0.05) delayed bacterial growth compared to neat CNF film. Minced pork wrapped with neat CNF film was classified as ‘spoiled (> 7 log CFU/mL)’ after 12 days of storage, whereas minced pork wrapped with CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film maintained the TVA value of 6.96 log CFU/mL after 15 days of storage, indicating the antibacterial efficiency of the CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% Film. These findings demonstrate the promising potential of CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film for food preservation.

Off-flavors in meat products primarily result from lipid oxidation, which generates peroxides. PV reflects the presence of ROSs and primarily indicates the occurrence of primary oxidation, including oxidative rancidity. In contrast, ρ-AV is an indicator of secondary oxidation products responsible for off-flavors and odors. A commonly followed guideline is to keep PV within the range of 10–20 meq/kg of fat to prevent the development of rancidity flavor [63]. The PV of minced pork varied over 21 days storage period, ranging from 1.78 ± 2.22 meq/kg to 5.97 ± 1.21 meq/kg for neat CNF film and 0.38 ± 0.33 meq/kg to 3.52 ± 0.85 meq/kg for CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film (Fig. 8d). The PV of minced pork wrapped with neat CNF film remained higher than CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film throughout the entire storage period, and both packages were found to decrease after 9 days of storage. This phenomenon may occur due to the faster degradation of the produced hydroperoxides (proceeding lipid oxidation converts peroxide into secondary products), or its interaction with other compounds [64]. This result is consistence with the ρ-AV result shown in Fig. 8e. After storage for 9 days, a rapid increase in ρ-AV was observed concurrent with a decrease in PV. The ρ-AV of the meat wrapped with CNF/Zn-GFP-CD films increased from an initial value of 3.19 ± 0.25 to 22.36 ± 1.85 over 21 days of storage, which was well below the maximum acceptable ρ-AV limit of 30 [65]. In contrast, the ρ-AV of the meat wrapped with neat CNF film increased to 31.76 ± 6.13 over the same 21 days storage period. The TBARS assay is one of the most established and commonly employed methods to quantify the lipid peroxidation end product MDA, a reactive aldehyde produced during peroxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids and considered to be the major end product of lipid peroxidation [66]. The maximum limit for TBARS values was set at 1.5 mg MDA/kg [67]. As shown in Fig. 8f, the initial TBARS of minced pork was approximately 0.25 mg MDA/kg, but increased rapidly to approximately 2.05 mg MDA/kg and 1.05 mg MDA/kg after 21 days in minced pork wrapped with neat CNF film and CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film, respectively. This result indicates the efficacy of CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film in protecting minced pork from lipid oxidation.

GFP was used to prepare GFP-CDs using a hydrothermal method. These GFP-CDs exhibited enhanced antioxidant and antibacterial properties upon zinc doping. Zn-GFP-CDs was successfully incorporated into CNF-based multifunctional composite films. These Zn-GFP-CDs are uniformly dispersed within the CNF matrix, providing transparency and effective UV-blocking properties. CNF/Zn-GFP-CDs showed remarkable antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Additionally, the a-value of minced pork wrapped with CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film was significantly lower than that of meat wrapped with neat CNF film, which suggests that CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film has UV-blocking and antioxidant properties. Additionally, wrapping minced pork with CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film significantly reduced bacterial growth, peroxide value, ρ-anisidine value, and MDA contents. These multifunctional CNF/Zn-GFP-CD5% film shows a great potential for active food packaging applications.
